schema.name: Taypro MINY Rooftop Solar Cleaning Robot
schema.description: Compact waterless solar panel cleaning robot for commercial and industrial rooftop arrays in India.

hero.title: Solar panel cleaning robot for rooftop — commercial & industrial
hero.subtitle: Utility-scale robots do not fit warehouse roofs and factory terraces. Taypro MINY is a compact waterless cleaning robot built for commercial rooftop solar — limited access, smaller arrays, and C&I economics.

cards.heading: Why standard utility robots fail on commercial rooftops
cards.subheading: Rooftop buyers search differently from 100 MW IPPs. These constraints drive product selection.

card0.title: Limited roof access
card0.body: Narrow stairwells, freight lifts, and no crane access mean full-size GLYDE-class robots cannot mobilise on most C&I sites.

card1.title: Smaller array footprints
card1.body: 1–5 MW rooftop portfolios need a robot sized for shorter rows and irregular layouts — not 300 MW block geometry.

card2.title: Building operations conflict
card2.body: Cleaning must run without disrupting warehouse logistics, HVAC intakes, or daytime building traffic.

card3.title: Water restrictions
card3.body: Many commercial rooftops prohibit wet-wash runoff. Waterless dry cleaning removes tanker logistics entirely.

card4.title: Safety and building operations
card4.body: Robotic cleaning runs on scheduled night windows without daytime warehouse traffic conflict. Operators follow building safety protocols and roof load limits assessed during feasibility.

card5.title: Soiling on urban rooftops
card5.body: Urban particulate, bird droppings, and industrial emissions create different soiling chemistry than desert utility plants — cycle planning must match local dust, not generic desert assumptions.

products.heading: Taypro robots for rooftop and distributed solar

product0.title: MINY — compact rooftop robot
product0.body: Purpose-built for commercial and industrial rooftops. Compact form factor, waterless dry cleaning, autonomous row traversal for C&I arrays.

product1.title: HELYX — semi-automatic for scattered blocks
product1.body: Pick-and-place semi-automatic robot for distributed portfolios with multiple small blocks where full autonomy is not economical.

product2.title: NECTYR monitoring
product2.body: Fleet scheduling and cleaning reports for multi-site C&I portfolios — even when robots move between blocks.

prose.heading: Who should evaluate a rooftop solar cleaning robot?
prose.p1: Commercial building owners, warehouse operators, manufacturing plants, and C&I solar developers with 500 kW–5 MW rooftop arrays are the primary buyers for MINY-class robots. If your portfolio is ground-mount utility scale, GLYDE or GLYDE-X is the better fit.
prose.p2: Taypro assesses roof load, access path, row length, and safety constraints before recommending MINY vs HELYX vs managed cleaning service. Not every rooftop qualifies for autonomous robots — we will tell you honestly after site review.
prose.p3: Compare MINY specifications and request a rooftop feasibility assessment from Taypro's engineering team.

faq.heading: Rooftop solar cleaning robot — FAQ
faq.subheading: Questions from C&I solar owners and facility managers.
faq.q0: What is the best solar cleaning robot for rooftop installations?
faq.a0: Taypro MINY is designed specifically for commercial and industrial rooftops — compact, waterless, and autonomous. For scattered multi-block C&I portfolios, HELYX semi-automatic may be more economical.
faq.q1: Can GLYDE or NYUMA clean rooftop solar panels?
faq.a1: GLYDE and NYUMA are engineered for ground-mount utility rows. They are typically too large for standard commercial rooftop access. MINY is the recommended Taypro product for rooftop buyer intent.
faq.q2: What rooftop sizes qualify for MINY?
faq.a2: MINY targets commercial rooftops from roughly 500 kW to 5 MW depending on row geometry and access. Contact Taypro for a site-specific feasibility review.
faq.q3: Does rooftop robotic cleaning use water?
faq.a3: No. Taypro robots use waterless dry cleaning — important for buildings with runoff restrictions and water-scarce locations.
faq.q4: Is MINY available in India now?
faq.a4: MINY is on Taypro's product roadmap with early-access deployments. Register interest on the MINY product page for commercial availability in your region.
faq.q5: How much does a rooftop cleaning robot cost?
faq.a5: Rooftop robot economics differ from utility CAPEX bands. Request a quote through the MINY product page or contact form with your array size and location.
faq.q6: Can Taypro provide managed cleaning on rooftops?
faq.a6: OPEX is primarily engineered for utility-scale ground-mount plants. Smaller C&I sites may qualify for robot CAPEX or HELYX programmes after feasibility review.
faq.a7: Share rooftop capacity, building type, access constraints, and photos of the array layout. Taypro will recommend MINY, HELYX, or alternative approaches.
faq.q8: What building types use rooftop cleaning robots in India?
faq.a8: Warehouses, logistics parks, manufacturing facilities, shopping malls, and institutional campuses with 500 kW+ rooftop arrays are primary adopters.
faq.q9: Can MINY clean around rooftop obstacles?
faq.a9: Feasibility depends on row layout, gap widths, and safety zones. Taypro maps paths during site assessment — irregular arrays may need HELYX pick-and-place instead.
faq.q10: Is wet cleaning ever used on rooftops?
faq.a10: Taypro robots are waterless by design — avoiding runoff, load addition, and building code issues common with pressure washing.
faq.q11: How does rooftop robot ROI compare to manual cleaning?
faq.a11: Manual rooftop cleaning requires safety harness crews, insurance, and building access coordination — often more expensive per kW than ground-mount manual. Run the ROI calculator with your tariff and soiling rate.

cta.body: Tell us about your commercial rooftop array. Taypro will recommend the right compact robot or alternative approach.

stats.heading: Commercial rooftop solar cleaning — market context

card0.label: Warehouses, factories, malls
card0.body: India's C&I rooftop solar exceeds 20 GW — most arrays are 500 kW to 5 MW where compact robots beat utility-scale machines.

card1.label: MINY form factor
card1.body: Designed for freight lifts and narrow roof hatches — GLYDE-class robots cannot mobilise on typical commercial rooftops.

card2.label: Waterless dry cleaning
card2.body: Building codes and runoff restrictions often prohibit wet-wash on industrial rooftops.

card3.label: Multi-block C&I portfolios
card3.body: Semi-automatic pick-and-place for scattered blocks where full autonomy is not economical.

steps.heading: How to evaluate a rooftop solar cleaning robot
steps.step0Body: Share kW/MW capacity, row length, tilt, lift dimensions, stair access, and photos. Taypro determines MINY vs HELYX feasibility.
steps.step1Body: Structural and safety constraints verified before robot deployment. Not every rooftop qualifies — Taypro advises honestly.
steps.step2Body: MINY compact robot for autonomous rows, or HELYX for pick-and-place scattered blocks. NECTYR optional for multi-site portfolios.
steps.step3Body: On-site training for building facilities team or Taypro operator placement depending on commercial model.
steps.step4Body: Typically 2–6 dry cycles monthly for urban C&I soiling — adjusted after first month PR observation.

eligibility.title: Is a rooftop cleaning robot right for your building?
eligibility.for0: Commercial/industrial rooftops 500 kW–5 MW with regular row geometry
eligibility.for1: Warehouses, manufacturing plants, logistics parks with night cleaning windows
eligibility.for2: Buildings prohibiting wet-wash runoff or water tanker access
eligibility.for3: C&I developers managing multi-site portfolios needing NECTYR reporting
eligibility.not0: Ground-mount utility plants (use GLYDE, GLYDE-X, or NYUMA instead)
eligibility.not1: Roofs failing structural or access feasibility review
eligibility.not2: Single small arrays where manual cleaning cost is already negligible

highlight.heading: Utility robots do not fit warehouse roofs.
highlight.lead: Rooftop buyer intent needs a compact solar cleaning robot — not a repackaged ground-mount GLYDE.
highlight.body: Taypro MINY is purpose-built for commercial rooftop solar in India. HELYX covers scattered multi-block C&I where pick-and-place beats full autonomy.

proseSecondary.heading: Commercial rooftop solar cleaning — warehouses, factories, and industrial parks
proseSecondary.p1: Warehouse operators with 1–3 MW flat roofs face the same soiling losses as utility plants — but cannot deploy 300 kg ground-mount robots through a freight lift. MINY-class compact robots address access, row length, and waterless constraints simultaneously.
proseSecondary.p2: Manufacturing plants with partial shading, HVAC exhaust, and bird activity need higher cleaning cadence than annual manual washes. Autonomous night cycles maintain PR without disrupting daytime logistics.
proseSecondary.p3: For multi-building C&I portfolios, NECTYR schedules robots across sites and exports cleaning evidence for sustainability reporting and O&M audits.
